OTHER POTENTIAL TITLES:

Automatic single-cell phenotyping detects predictive microenvironment
features in ovarian cancer

Tribus single-cell phenotyping method finds X and Y features do Z in
ovarian cancer

Tribus reveals the single-cell tumor architecture and effect of
chemotherapy in ovarian cancer

**Angela Szabo^1\*^, Teodora Faragó^1\*^, Inga-Maria Launonen^1^,
Jia-Ren Lin^2^, Kevin Elias^5^, Peter Sorger^2^, Julia Casado^1,^
Anniina Färkkilä^1,3,4^**

**^1^** Research Programs Unit, Faculty of Medicine, University of
Helsinki, Finland. **^2^** Laboratory of Systems Pharmacology, Harvard
Medical School, United States. **^3^** Department of obstetrics and
gynecology, Helsinki University Hospital, Finland. **^4^** iCAN digital
precision medicine flagship, Finland. **^5^** Department of Obstetrics
Gynecology & Repro. Bio., Brigham and Women\'s Hospital, United States.
Correspondence should be addressed to A.F.
(anniina.farkkila@helsinki.fi).

**Multiplexed imaging at single-cell resolution is becoming increasingly
useful to decipher the role of cellular microenvironment in cancer and
other complex diseases. To identify spatial patterns of single cells on
a tissue we must first assign accurate descriptions to each cell in a
step known as *cell-type phenotyping*. This step is challenging due to
(i) laborious annotation of ground truth, (ii) segmentation artifacts,

curate descriptions to each cell in a
step known as *cell-type phenotyping*. This step is challenging due to
(i) laborious annotation of ground truth, (ii) segmentation artifacts,
(iii) fluorescence noise and batch effects, and (iv) difficulty to
reproduce human-biased thresholding. Here we present *Tribus*, an
interactive, *knowledge-based classifier* that avoids hard-set
thresholds and manual labeling, is robust to noise, and takes less
iterations from the user than standard labeling of clustering results.
Interactive analysis is done via a Napari plugin, and each analysis
creates a detailed report to enable reproducibility. In this study we
show that *Tribus* compares to human knowledge in public benchmarking
datasets where manual cell type annotations are supported by the
pathology community. We applied *Tribus* on a dataset consisting of
cyclic immunofluorescence (CyCIF) images of six matched ovarian cancer
samples collected before and after neoadjuvant chemotherapy. Accurate
cell-type phenotyping enabled a high resolution analysis of cellular
phenotypes and their spatial patterns, as well as their temporal
dynamics during platinum-taxane chemotherapy. Tribus - an easily
embeddable open-source package - thus enables accurate phenotyping of
single cells to facilitate biological discovery from highly multiplexed
images.**

## Tissue architecture of treatment-naive ovarian cancer samples

First, we performed Tribus analysis on the PRE samples. Diagnosis
samples are widely studied, we expect to see the same findings as

## Tissue landmarks before and after chemotherapy

Besides cell type calling, Tribus also extracts common useful spatial
features given the X and Y coordinates. This produces quantifiable
tissue landmarks that can be then analyzed using standard data analysis
functions.

(Figure 3)

# DISCUSSION

Cell type calling and feature extraction is a critical step in
high-dimensional image analysis.

There are different challenges in bioimage analysis than in sorted cell
suspension-based methods like scRNA-seq or CyTOF.

Prior knowledge can be a curse and a blessing and we need the quality
assessment of the channels to not bias the results with our own
expectations.
